title: Trump ties U.S.-Saudi nuclear deal to Israeli recognition
dek: President Trump announced Thursday that the U.S.-Saudi civil nuclear cooperation agreement is contingent on Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords and recognizing Israel. The White House states the deal will be withdrawn if Saudi Arabia refuses the condition. Saudi Arabia has previously declared it will not recognize Israel without Palestinian statehood.
author: The GoCheckMyNews Desk
key_fact: Trump stated on Truth Social the nuclear deal is 'totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords.'
bottom_line: Trump has publicly linked the nuclear deal to a diplomatic condition Saudi Arabia has explicitly rejected before. The test comes with congressional review: if the actual deal text either allows uranium enrichment or does not include the Abraham Accords condition, Trump's leverage evaporates and the broader regional strategy unravels.
body: President Trump announced on Thursday that the U.S.-Saudi civil nuclear cooperation agreement, signed Wednesday, is conditional on Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords and recognizing Israel. Trump wrote on Truth Social: "The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)... will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords."

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed the condition, stating Trump "has said if they don't join the Abraham Accords, the deal is off," according to BBC News. Leavitt said Trump had discussed the requirement "on numerous previous occasions" with Saudi leaders. The nuclear deal was described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy programme."

Saudi Arabia has previously said it will not recognize Israel without the establishment of a Palestinian state, per BBC reporting. That long-standing position directly contradicts the requirement Trump has now imposed. The Abraham Accords have led to five countries, four of them Arab, recognizing Israel, according to the BBC. During Trump's first term, the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain signed the accords, resulting in the first formal Israeli-Arab peace agreements in decades. Sudan, Morocco, and Kazakhstan have since joined.

The actual nuclear deal text has not been released publicly, and it remains unclear whether the Abraham Accords condition is written into the agreement or imposed unilaterally by Trump as a prerequisite for congressional approval. BBC reporting notes it is "not immediately clear if the Abraham Accords condition was stipulated in the text of the nuclear deal... or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition."

Trump's statement claims the deal contains a provision that "There will be no enrichment of material." However, earlier U.S. media reporting suggested the deal could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future, which can be used as fuel for power plants but can also potentially be used to make nuclear weapons. Rosemary Kelanic, director of the Middle East programme at the Defense Priorities think tank, called uranium enrichment precedent "quite shocking," stating: "The US has never done that before. We have never helped another country enrich on their own soil," according to BBC News. Kelanic added that enrichment capability means "if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk" for building nuclear weapons.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office posted on social media that Saudi Arabia joining the accords "would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East." Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said previously his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means. Israel is widely believed to have nuclear weapons but neither confirms nor denies this.

Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman told CBS News in 2018 that his nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but stated: "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible."

The agreement will be sent to the U.S. Congress for review. Some lawmakers on both sides of the political aisle are expected to oppose the deal, though Trump's Republican Party controls both chambers and opponents do not appear to have the votes needed to block it, according to BBC News.
Saudi Arabia has not yet responded to Trump's remarks.
